flashcards.front: What is the basic principle of Insertion Sort?
flashcards.back: Insertion Sort builds a sorted array one element at a time by repeatedly taking the next element and inserting it into its correct position in the already sorted portion.

flashcards.front: When is Insertion Sort most efficient?
flashcards.back: Insertion Sort is most efficient when sorting small datasets (n < 50) or nearly sorted arrays. In these cases, it can approach O(n) time complexity.

flashcards.front: Is Insertion Sort a stable sorting algorithm?
flashcards.back: Yes, Insertion Sort is stable because it does not change the relative order of elements with equal keys.

flashcards.front: What does it mean that Insertion Sort is an 'online' algorithm?
flashcards.back: An online algorithm can process its input piece-by-piece as it arrives, without needing the entire input from the start. Insertion Sort can sort data as it receives it.

flashcards.front: How does Insertion Sort handle duplicate elements?
flashcards.back: Insertion Sort maintains the relative order of duplicate elements (stable sort), so duplicates will appear in their original order in the sorted output.
